author: @jacobbesler3214
text:
Wicked really does feel like a highschool fanfic that somehow made it to Broadway. It has the classic hallmarks:
- Core storyline involves two students forced together then apart by the roles society gave them and their own unexpectedly significant choices
- Greater-scope conflict straight out of a freshman's intro to ethics and geopolitics
- Worldbuilding has some interesting ideas but was clearly not fully thought out
- Romantic interest added for the author's favorite character who is perfect for her but still ends up in a love triangle
- Bloat caused by increasingly desperate callbacks to source material that is already incompatible with the new story
- Whole thing is more heavy-handed than a pair of osmium mittens

replies.author: @BertoxolusThePuzzled
replies.text:
​@themonsterunderyourbed9408Yes, but that is in the context of opposing the Wizard, who is established as a massive liar...

It is is entirely believable that the Wizard as presented in screen would absolutely run a massive smear campaign against a neighboring ruler that he saw as being in his way. Not that that's what happened of course,  but it opens the door for the Wicked Witch being wicked because she opposes the Wizard while his ally the "good" Witch is called good BECAUSE she's his ally. I mean it's not like Glinda comes off as a "good" person given that she could have sent Dorothy home all the way in the beginning and instead manipulated her into joining the Wizards faction and being sent to wipe out their primary political rival before FINALLY teaching her how to go home.

I mean just think about it, Glinda presumably ends up as queen of not just the two territories of the Wicked Witches but also presumably running the Emerald City itself with the Wizard now gone. She's basically set herself up to be empress of basically all of Oz, whether intentionally via manipulation or unintentionally via luck.

Now I am not saying that this was the original intention of the movie because it wasn't, but there is certainly enough room in the established narrative to go in that direction. Granted Wicked goes a bit off the rails in the final act as it starts retconning more and more stuff but the basic idea and first half or so of the story setting up the backstory works quite well all things considered.

(at) BertoxolusThePuzzled

When the Wicked witch first meets Dorothy (in Munchkinland), she does _not_ believe that Dorothy unalived her sister. Instead, she believes that mysterious circumstances caused a house to fall on her (mysterious because she may not have known about the tornado, since tornadoes may be a rare phenomenon in Ozempic). Although the Munchkins themselves did think that Dorothy might be a witch (guess it was so long after the _Wizard_ arrived in Ozempic that the Munchkins totally forgot that tornadoes can happen), Glinda correctly realized that Dorothy was _not_ a witch (read = she had no magic). We can assume that the Wicked witch is as equally intelligent, & therefore _also_ knew that Dorothy was not capable of dropping a house on somebody, since that would require Dorothy to have magic, & the Wicked witch knew that she did _not_ have any magic. Even if _anybody had_ thought that Dorothy was a witch, nobody would've that _anybody_ could control a tornado. They knew their magic's limits.

That is _not_ speculation. It is corroborated by the Wicked witch telling Dorothy that the magic slippers were useless to Dorothy, which she would not have _bothered_ to tell Dorothy IF she thought that Dorothy had magic. (The Wicked witch may have thought that she was telling Dorothy the _truth_ about the magic slippers being useless to Dorothy, since the Wicked witch probably didn't know that Dorothy would want to (magically) leave Ozempic.)

​@mateuszsawicki6495

I'd say it's more that we currently have a bit too much of "the villain is misunderstood and the hero is just as bad."

Which is fine, culture is a pendulum and we'll have to swing back the other way for a while, then come back to where we are now.

I'd say the biggest critique of "the villain is misunderstood" is that usually it doesn't lead to any cathartic storytelling most of the time. It just takes what you know, specifically in this case, and goes "what if original story but the opposite?" and that can be a thematic and storytelling dead-end because the original is usually written the way it was for a reason.

Keep something in mind:

This is an alternative take to the story. Much like the 1939 movie was an alternative take. I held the same opinion because I thought Wicked was trying to make it seem like the Wicked Witch was the secret good guy all along. In reality, Wicked is basically a “what if?” story the sort of melds the original Wizard of Oz books and the 1939 movie.

Once you realize that it’s not them intentionally undoing a story and its characters but instead puts them in an “alternative universe”, the premise becomes a bit easier to swallow. So the 1939 movie and Wicked are essentially two separate entities. There’s just enough crossover so Wicked can connect its overarching plot.

text: Here's an example of the tonal shift in adaptation. In the stage play, Elphaba rhetorically asks Glinda, "Who steals shoes off a dead woman?!" replies.text: Theater, I think, also provides more of a "distance" between the stage and the audience, because you're more aware that you're simply seeing actors playing out a scene.  Film is more immersive, because you don't get to see actors or stagehands entering or leaving from the side of the stage, there's no obvious pause between scenes in different locations as the sets are moved around, and so on.  That was one reason they changed the ending to the film version of the "Little Shop of Horrors" musical -- test audiences reacted negatively to Audrey and Seymour dying at the end, because the "death" of those characters felt too real, whereas in the stage play the audience accepted it because they knew it *wasn't* real and the actors would all come out to take a bow at the end.

text:
Honestly, I saw the movie yesterday. While it wasn't incoherent garbage, the pacing was BIZARRE at times. Like, they try tiptoeing around the OG WoO's events in the back third... but they also have events that ABSOLUTELY SHOULD have been recognized and altered what happened.

The Tin Man screaming about how Elphaba turned him to tin, destroyed his heart, and now he wants to kill her? With only the Lion next to him? Where the fuck were the Scarecrow and Dorothy?

I saw that last act and thought, "fuck, it would have been interesting if they had the balls to actually address more." Instead Dorothy doesn't even get the character depth of the Wicked book, where her innocence and genuine sympathies for her sister sends Elphaba into a mental BSOD.

There's a lot to praise, sure, but FUCK was it a weird movie.

There is actual room within the original narrative to argue that the Wicked Witch was mostly justified and that most of her wickedness was of the cartoonish variety as opposed to the truly evil variety (IE she didn't use magic to give them a plague or have her flying monkeys kill them on sight for revenge, she just wanted her magical family shoes back).

She was evil sure but not irredeemably evil, in the sense that she is implied to be a tyrant but is also absolutely someone you could normally make a rational deal with and expect her to stick to the terms. That combined with the her primary enemy the Wizard being an expert conman all gives her character enough leeway for something like Wicked, granted the later bits do go a bit off the rails in terms of sticking to established canon.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
A lot of things are a cheap "what if everything you were told was wrong". It's been done. I'm sure there are works that are based on reading the bible and thinking "I want to know more about this satan guy" and then writing a fan fiction about how he was unfairly maligned. It's a creative daring idea the first time you do it. But the impulse is weird. So many works are trading on existing works that they're corrupting. The Acolyte tried to do "what if the Jedi were evil?". Maleficent. Cruella. Doing it again and again and again seems pathological, uncreative and corrosive. Sooner or later, someone is going to do a musical production of the life of a German Chancellor of the 1930s and how he was really just a misunderstood character and framed by evil Mr Churchill out of envy.

Trying to use an existing story alters the original. If you keep it goofy, then you limit your level of arrogance. But this is going to be a lot of children's first exposure to Oz. It's effectively replacing the original.

There are other paths to desecation. Viewing old works or even historical events as fodder for cheap fictions has a similar disease. So Margot Robbie desecrating Wuthering Heights might be a similar lack of respect. There's an audacity or postmodern glibness to touching something you have no right to or don't understand. it's like trying to create a sequel to Hamlet. 'Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead' kept a level of distance. But you'd have to at least consider the hubris of touching the earlier work or trying to piggyback off it. - And how self serving it is.

replies.author: @DearlyDepartedDaz
replies.text: The two are not comparable. Cruella is about how the actions of the villain were justified and reasonable from her perspective. Wicked is about how The Wizard of Oz is a bunch of lies that never actually happened. It's not a reversal of good and evil if what you thought was good was actually evil all along. Like a corporation you thought was good being exposed as having used slave labor the entire time. Do you still think they're were a good company when the truth comes out?

author: @Darkgun231
text:
I actually read the book. I had no idea what it was about. I thought it was one the official continuations to the Oz story, so for a long time I was under the impression it was considered canon to the Oz-verse. 

Honestly? It was okay. Very much a 90's early 2000s sort of book. And looking back at it, it did read as fanfic (I had yet to discover that rabbithole for myself), but it wasn't the worst thing I ever read. Certainly better than a few things I ended up consuming. But finding out that the story is not considered part of the 14 books Frank Baum personally wrote, or any of the others later written by his estate? That was a surprise, and it did feel like a bit of a betrayal. 

Overall? The book is, at best, average. It tries too hard to make the world of Oz make sense from modern perspective and sensibilities rather than just let the fantasy stuff be fantasy stuff.

Haven't seen the movies or stageplays, but I'm not a fan of musicals so I think I'll just skip 'em.
